LP memo June 2026 · San Francisco

Babylon

the cultural investor on a technical cap table.

A $15M pre-seed fund · Hugo Amsellem · San Francisco

TLDR: capital is becoming a commodity, and you win a seat on the best frontier cap tables by being memorable and doing the one thing for a founder that the big platforms structurally can't. For the companies I back, that thing is the cultural layer. I'm the only investor in the room whose entire job is turning hard technology into culture — the narrative, the founder's positioning, the public imagination, the hiring magnetism, the fundraising story, the creators who carry it. I work as an artisan: small checks, deep work, no board seats, in person, in San Francisco. The fund is $15M, around 35 companies, ~2% entry ownership, no reserves. The discipline is ownership; check size flexes to hold it.

You've watched some of this already. Intuition — my first fund, the lab — is ~$7.5M across ~40 companies and 76 LPs, and the second half of that book is already marking up faster than the first: Eridale ~6.25x, Eigen ~4.3x, Minerva ~4.1x, with Founders Fund, OpenAI, Sequoia, Benchmark and General Catalyst pricing my early calls a few months after I made them. Babylon is what happens when I take everything that book taught me and build the fund I actually should have built from the start — the right thesis, in the right city, at the right size, with the right construction. Everything below is the why.

Part one
the thesis
I.

Culture is moving upstream of technology

AGI is arriving into a philosophical vacuum, and it opens a crisis on two levels at once. For the tiny sliver of people who can build anything — the top fraction of a percent of technical talent — it's an existential question: what is worth a life's work when the machine can do the work too. For nearly everyone else, it's a nihilistic one: what is my purpose when my labor, and maybe my judgment, is no longer the point. Meaning becomes the scarce resource.

That is why culture is moving upstream of technology. For fifty years it ran the other way — we built things because we could, and culture adapted downstream. Liberalism gave us a clean way to avoid arguing about good and bad, so we outsourced the question to two neutral arbiters, the market and the law: do people want it, is it legal. Technology became an autonomous force with no inherent direction, just capability in search of demand. That arrangement is breaking, for a structural reason: networked publics now have real veto power over what gets adopted. Data centers get blocked. Nuclear gets killed. Gene editing gets frozen. A single viral moment can close a regulatory pathway that took a decade to open.

So in the world we've entered, storytelling matters almost as much as engineering — because four constituencies have to believe in a company before there's any proof it works. Two are internal: the talent that has its pick of where to go, and the capital that decides who gets funded. Two are external: the regulators who grant or withhold permission, and the public whose trust can be pulled overnight. Winning all four is a narrative problem before it is a technical one.

I wrote one essay making this argument and it did half a million impressions. The signal was who reposted it: Sam Lessin (GP at Slow Ventures), Garry Tan (CEO of Y Combinator), Akshay Kothari (founder of Notion), Sriram Krishnan (AI at the White House), Taylor Lorenz, and Antonio García Martínez — people who set the weather in this town, all feeling the same shift. A genuine, non-performative cultural obsession turns out to be wildly differentiating in San Francisco, which, after ten months here, is the thing I least expected and now rely on most.

II.

The dual frontier — a new category

This is why the best founders now win on two frontiers at once.

The technical frontier is the obvious one: build something real, fast enough that the next model or the next competitor doesn't erase it. Stand still and you get commoditized. The cultural frontier is the one almost no one underwrites: win the talent war, move the capital, keep the regulators open, and earn the public's permission — all before there's proof. Both have to advance together now, and on frontier companies the cultural one increasingly decides who wins. That dual frontier is the whole thesis.

And the cultural frontier gets won with creators now, far more than journalists. PR is dead. The public is unbullshittable, and legacy media has lost the trust that made traditional PR work. Founders go direct, build their own voice, and assemble allies who have skin in the game — people who can't jump to the next outlet after a blowup, because their audience is their accountability. Science YouTubers who make the work legible. Post-strike Hollywood writers who can build a founding myth that doesn't read like a deck. Industrial designers who give the story a physical body. Substackers and new-media operators who run founder-led media independent of the press. I call it the narrative gang.

III.

The cultural seat, and why it's mine

Once you accept the dual frontier, the question for a fund gets simple: on a technical cap table, who owns the cultural frontier? Almost no one. A lead carries everything that makes a company — hiring, GTM, governance, the grind — and deserves every point of the 10–15% they take for it. But each of the four constituencies that have to believe before there's proof is a narrative problem, and the seat responsible for them is almost always empty. I fill it. Because it's the only thing I do, I can be the best in the world at it.

What that looks like in practice: I run the cultural frontier across a founder's whole journey — the founding story, the talent magnetism, the fundraising arc, the regulatory permission, the public imagination — built from day one with the same intention as the product. A narrative operating system.

narrative is load-bearing infrastructure for these companies — as essential as the engineering stack, and sometimes more urgent to get right first.

And almost no one else can sit in this seat, because I've spent fifteen years on the seam between engineers and storytellers — on both sides of it — with one obsession running through all of it: helping people scale without permission. Musicians past the labels. Creators past the networks. Founders past the gatekeepers.

I started as a founder. I built Oocto, a decentralized music label in Europe that helped artists route around the major labels and own their audience directly — years before anyone said "creator economy." It made me early and native to the informational paradigm that now governs everything: permissionless distribution, audience as leverage, the artist (and now the founder) as the channel. The thesis I run today is a direct descendant of it.

Then I helped redefine what entrepreneurship felt like in Europe. As part of the founding team of The Family, I took over pre-seed in Paris and helped open Berlin and London, and for seven years I worked with pre-seed founders all day, every day — building the largest founder fellowship in Europe and backing companies like Algolia, PayFit, and Qonto on their way to becoming billion-dollar businesses. Seven years of it is why backing the overlooked, pre-consensus founder is pure instinct for me now.

Then I went all the way back to the creator side. A few essays I wrote about the creator economy went viral, and I became one of its meta-creators, genuinely part of the world I was writing about. That took me to Jellysmack, then the leading company in the creator economy, backed by a billion-dollar round from SoftBank, where I started its creator fund — investing directly in creators — and then worked on M&A. I learned, from the inside, how storytellers actually build, monetize, and defend an audience.

So the reason I can hold the cultural seat is simple, and hard to copy: I'm genuinely a peer in both rooms. A peer to founders, because I've been one and have spent fifteen years in the trenches with them. A peer to creators, because I've been one too — a Substacker myself, inside that world the whole time. Almost no one comes from the creator world and credibly points it at the frontier; I've spent years building trust across that exact intersection, and it's why I'm close to alone in it. The clearest tell: I'm one of the only venture people speaking at Open Sauce, the leading science-creator conference, this July — the rare investor on a stage full of creators.

Part two
Babylon
IV.

From investing in culture to investing with culture

I've already been running a version of this, and the cleanest way to understand Babylon is to watch what changed inside Intuition — my first fund, the lab — because the book splits almost perfectly in two.

I started Intuition with a co-founder and a consumer thesis, and that first half of the book was the gestation of something far more ambitious — a thesis that took real reps, and a year on the ground in the US, to come into full focus. I was all the way in; the idea simply grew larger than where it started. I went solo in the spring of 2025; there's no conflict in the story — we're still close, and he's off building — and going solo is what finally let me point the whole thing where it was always headed.

The first half was investing in culture: consumer companies. The second half is investing with culture — a cultural lens, a cultural layer, on frontier companies. The second half is more me in every dimension, thesis and personality and performance, and it's marking up faster than the first despite being a year younger. That is the entire argument for Babylon, and it's already on the page.

That page is short, and it's just the preamble — three facts that tell you Babylon is a bet I've half-made already. I built a few genuinely great companies, most of them after going solo. The best funds in the world have already marked them up. And I raised the whole thing from top LPs.

~$7.5M·~40 companies·76 LPs·pre-seed

A handful are marking up hard — each mark a top fund pricing my earliest call up, months after I made it. The strongest all landed after I went solo:

CompanyWhat it isCo-investorsMark
Eridaleautonomous kitchens for franchise-scale restaurantsFounders Fund, OpenAI6.25x
Eigenmultiplayer AI social protocolBenchmark4.3x
Orionsmart-cooling bed at half Eight Sleep's priceMucker, Second Sight4.2x
Minervahumanoids for hazardous operationsGeneral Catalyst4.1x
Klipyrev-share API for GIFs, stickers & AI-memesGoogle's AI fund3.5x
DeductionAI-native personal CPA automating tax prepCreator Ventures2.9x
Apollo Atomicscompact nuclear reactorsYC (demo day incoming)2.3x
Delphidigital cloning platform for knowledge workersSequoia, Founders Fund, Lux2.0x
Phase Labsfocused-ultrasound device for tissue regenerationInitialized, Long Journey1.7x
Sparkmatemodular electric steel millsSNR, Afore1.7x
Select markups across Intuition — the five strongest all landed after going solo.

I'm not going to invent a TVPI, so here's the honest texture: these are SAFE marks, not realized returns. But that's the signal, not the caveat — premier funds like Founders Fund, OpenAI and General Catalyst writing $7–8M SAFE rounds is exactly the validation, and far more common in the US than in Europe. The best investors in the world keep pricing my earliest calls up within months of my making them: Sequoia followed me into Delphi, Benchmark into Eigen, Founders Fund and OpenAI into Eridale, and General Catalyst into Minerva.

The LPs are the same caliber on the other side of the table — top funds (General Catalyst, Goodwater, Tiny VC, Kima, Masia), top founders (Deel, Vinted, Zalando, Jellysmack), top European GPs (Cherry, Credo, 20VC, Highland, Creator Ventures), and top cultural leaders (Raphaël Varane of Real Madrid, Cyril Gane of the UFC, Pablo Longoria of Olympique de Marseille, and Nas Daily).

Babylon is that second half — named correctly, and built from scratch to do only this. In spirit it's my Fund I done over, with the mistake already paid for and the edge already proven. Here's what it backs, and how I win it.

V.

What Babylon backs

There's a kind of company that doesn't have a clean name yet, so instead of defining it I'll draw it. Put any frontier business on two axes — how hard the technology is to build, and how hard the surrounding culture is to win — and the ones that go on to create or dominate a category land in the same place: the corner where both run high at once. That corner is the dual frontier, and it's the whole map. It's also the map almost no fund is built around, which is the opening.

The line has two poles, and Babylon sits about two-thirds below it and a third above.

Below the line is the narrative-intensive frontier — roughly two-thirds of the fund. The technology is the steeper climb, but belief still gates everything: the talent, the capital, the regulators, the myth. The public here is the audience. Minerva builds humanoids for hazardous operations; Dynamo Air, an autonomous heavy-lift chinook; Apollo Atomics, compact nuclear reactors; Sparkmate, modular electric steel mills; Edgerun, an exoskeleton for heavy loads. None of them sells to consumers, and every one needs a founding myth — Edgerun and Apollo most of all, because exoskeletons and nuclear are pure science fiction the moment the story is told right.

Above the line is the consumer frontier — the other third. The engineering is genuinely hard, and the culture is harder still, because here the public is the user, adopting or rejecting the thing out loud. Eigen put a multiplayer AI social protocol in front of people; Orion sells a smart-cooling bed at half Eight Sleep's price; Acorn Genetics is a pocket DNA sequencer; Metabologic is an enzyme that turns the sugar you eat into allulose in your gut; Vight is a personal two-seat eVTOL; Eridale runs a restaurant from an autonomous kitchen. Every one is a real technical bet whose fate is decided by whether the public falls for it. I've written about the consumer frontier here.

At the top, where the two climbs meet at full intensity, are the companies as hard to make people believe as they are to build: Kangaroo Bio, artificial wombs from bioprinted endometrium, and Phase Labs, a focused-ultrasound device aimed at regenerating tissue — and, if it works, organs. These are the purest expression of the thesis, and the ones a cultural failure kills outright no matter how good the science. The animal gene-editing companies I wanted to back from Europe and couldn't, for legal reasons tied to where the fund sat, belong right here.

It's one thesis, and it stretches — from hard consumer plays, through sci-fi bets, to frontier science — held by a single claim: in every one of these, narrative is what turns a hard technology into a category. That is the company Babylon is built to back, and the map only widens from here.

In thesis
narrative-intensive frontier · consumer frontier · the peak where both max out (bio · robotics · frontier industrials · consumer tech with real cultural stakes)
Not my card
B2B SaaS · B2B AI · enterprise · AI infra & wrappers without cultural stakes · non-US

What I pass on follows from the same logic. B2B SaaS, B2B AI, and enterprise, where the value a small investor adds is customer intros and the narrative barely matters — a game I leave to others. AI infrastructure and wrappers with no cultural stakes. And anything not built for the US market. If culture won't be a core element of the business, I can't get into the best of the best, and I have no edge anyway — that's the whole bottom-left of the map.

Which leaves the obvious question: how does a cultural investor underwrite the frontier at all? It starts with a line I keep coming back to.

"deep tech" doesn't mean anything. software was deep tech twenty years ago. robotics was deep tech five years ago. bio is deep tech right now.

When a space commoditizes, defensibility shifts from raw technical difficulty to IP, brand, and network effects — the same way it works in content or fashion. What's underneath that shift is that AI changed who can build: the science on these frontiers is largely settled, and the hard part is now engineering it into the real world under real constraints. The crackest technical founders — the maniacs who'd have shipped software a decade ago — feel the commoditization first and move to where building is still the hard part: atoms, cells, reactors.

So I don't need to be a deep-tech investor. I need to recognize the builder, and that's the one thing fifteen years around founders has trained me to do on sight. I'm not underwriting the physics; I'm underwriting the person, and then I do the cultural work only I can do. Where I have to hold a real view on a niche, the move is sharp human taste plus AI to go down the rabbit hole and understand a new field fast — enough to have a point of view without being the smartest person in the room on the science.

VI.

The barbell — pre-consensus, and the artisan

Sourcing is commoditizing — better tools, AI, and the velocity of communication are pushing toward a world where most investors see most deals, and the best founders are increasingly obvious to all of them — so you no longer win on sourcing, or on the size of your wire. You win on what you uniquely bring to a founder who can raise money anywhere. That is reshaping venture into a barbell. On one end, the multi-stage industrialists — a16z, General Catalyst — competing on brand, balance sheet, and scaled services, and now moving aggressively earlier, writing the pre-seed checks that used to be beneath them. On the other end, human, artisanal specialists doing irrational things that don't scale. The undifferentiated middle is what commoditized capital and AI arbitrage away first.

I'm not competing with the industrialists, and I'm not trying to — that's simply not my game. I'm building the other pole, and I think it's the pole that wins pre-seed. Ryan Hoover put it cleanly: all that matters is winning — differentiation, human ability, being memorable, being at the center of everything, and building the right tools.

My edge inside that barbell is two things. The first is being memorable — having a real, distinctive opinion about the world, which a decade of writing has trained me to convey. That unapologetic authenticity, and the cultural obsession underneath it, is what makes me stand out, especially in San Francisco. The second is being early — pre-consensus. The mechanical reason a $15M fund can take 2% is that I'm in before the round is hot, on something a little heretical: a pre-consensus founder whose background doesn't fit the Stanford pattern-match, a pre-consensus geography, or a pre-consensus topic most capital still finds illegible. The moment everyone agrees a founder will win — the moment the industrialists can see it — they don't need me and I can't get meaningful ownership. The window where 2% lives — roughly $5M to $30M — is the window before consensus, and that's the only window I play in.

Being early gets me the price; the cultural work is what makes it pay. I make the company legible to everyone who comes next — the talent, the press, the funds that lead the next round — manufacturing the consensus that wasn't there when I wrote the first check. A GP I respect framed the whole new game in a line: the job is to manufacture consensus. It's been my job all along, and the markups in the table earlier are that consensus arriving on schedule.

Eridale, Minerva, and Eigen were one of each, and they show what being early on purpose actually looks like.

Eridale — the overlooked founder. French, overlooked at home; I'd backed his earlier company when he was eighteen, at The Family. He rebooted in the US building autonomous kitchens for franchise-scale restaurants. I wrote the first check at an $8M cap on a SAFE and introduced him to OpenAI, who did the deal; Founders Fund led the round at $30M. It's a 6.25x mark, and he just closed his next round at $50M. He wasn't legible to American capital at first — which was the arbitrage.
Minerva — the overlooked geography. Eastern European, young, and to me an inevitable founder, building teleoperated humanoids for dangerous environments. He came to me through my European network the moment he landed in San Francisco; I committed first, at roughly a $12M valuation, and then acted as his fundraising ally through the whole American conquest — working the narrative and the negotiation as the round climbed. General Catalyst came in at $30M and others at $50M.
Eigen — the overlooked topic. A German founder, completely peculiar, with total clarity on a topic most capital found illegible: multiplayer social AI — something I'd been writing about and was obsessed with, so I could read both his brilliance and the direction before consensus formed. I met him early in his raise, just after he landed in San Francisco — a first call, then dinner the same night — and committed on the spot. From there I orchestrated his introductions across San Francisco and worked closely with him to nail the narrative of his core hypothesis. I was in at $15M; Benchmark priced it at $60M a few weeks later.

Each one is the same move: early because I saw it, knew why, and earned the seat by showing up first and going to work. The markup is just the receipt.

Europe is the geography I have the deepest edge on. Europeans create more US unicorns per capita than Americans do — France runs about 45x the native-born rate — and they tend to be technically brilliant and unusually loyal on talent, especially across robotics and bio. What they lack is the narrative muscle the US rewards, which is exactly my help and exactly my network. They're arriving in San Francisco in record numbers, the crackest twenty-two-year-olds leaving the moment they can. I'd have caught them late from Paris; I catch them early here, while they still read as pre-consensus to American capital.

And the way I win those pre-consensus seats is by being an artisan, not an industrialist — so I'll be direct about how I build, because it's the most honest thing I can tell an LP. My parents make wine in the Rhône Valley. They're maybe a top-30 domaine in France, but tiny — a hundred thousand bottles, biodynamic, everything by hand, irrationally inefficient on purpose, built out of love for the craft. That's the fund I want to build. I'm not going to institutionalize it: no leading, no $50M Fund III and $100M Fund IV, no army of associates — a boutique, specialist vehicle that maybe reaches $25M at terminal size and keeps doing things for founders that bigger firms can't be bothered to do. The artisan's edge is the irrational extra mile, and the irrational extra mile is exactly what wins a seat the industrialist would never work hard enough to earn on a $200K check.

That craft is physical, and it's why I'm in San Francisco. New York shows up once a month, Europe once a quarter; I'm here every night. And I don't take board seats — it keeps me genuinely founder-friendly and easy to say yes to, and it keeps my options open on the way up, since without a board seat I'm far less restricted on when and how I can take liquidity, which matters more for returns than people admit.

VII.

How I win: creators, community, and content

I win the seat — and find the deals — through three things: creators, community, and content. They're the cultural frontier made operational, and they compound into each other.

Creators. I curate a standing community of creators whose job is to expand the narrative capacity of my portfolio companies: science creators who make hard work legible, top Substackers, new-media properties like podcasters, and a short list of agencies that specialize in helping frontier companies communicate. This is the narrative gang. My advantage here is hard to copy — I've been in the creator world for years, and I sit on the board of Influencers.club, which holds the largest and best creator data in the world, so I can find the exact creators who move a given audience, reach them directly, and orchestrate a campaign across them. Identifying and mobilizing the right creators is the hardest part of narrative work, and I've turned it into infrastructure.

Community. I run narrative-first fundraising for these founders, especially the Europeans my network brings over, and I do it in rooms I build myself. I don't sponsor my way into other people's; I host my own and obsess over the physical details until the vibe can't be faked. Three rooms do most of the work, and they compound into each other.

The Babylon Banquet is the heartbeat. Once a month I sit twenty-five to thirty of the best founders, investors, and creators in San Francisco at a single long table — and I mean a table I built. I worked out the exact width a shared table needs to feel intimate instead of corporate, had the wooden planks cut to spec at a specialist shop, and set them on trestles into one continuous banquet that seats thirty in my office: candles down the center, colorful plates shared family-style, no agenda. Fully loaded it runs me under $1,000 a night, with almost no outside help — which is the entire point. The standard San Francisco dinner is underwritten by a bank or a law firm, and that sponsor quietly kills the intention and the vibe. Mine has no sponsor, no logos, no pitch. It's irrationally intentional, and that's exactly why the right people keep coming back — and why founders, talent, and creators connect here in a way no panel or demo day reproduces.

The Epic of… is the cultural frontier made public. I run it in a friend's event space in the Mission, so it costs almost nothing to put on, and I only interview Series B founders who are the purest expression of the dual frontier — and I interview them as the authors of a myth, not the subjects of a fireside. The questions are about the narrative, the near-cultlike conviction, the founder's almost-religious belief in the work, the cultural intention behind it — in front of a curated crowd of sixty to eighty founders and top talent, recorded and published to X and YouTube afterward. The first guest just raised a major round from a16z. And from July, every guest gets a custom-printed poster of their company, handed out from a merch stand built for the night — a level of taste San Francisco hasn't seen at an event like this.

European Sundays is the landing pad. The first year in San Francisco is brutal for a European founder, and my job is to make it short. I run this one as a gang — alongside excellent European solo GPs now based here, one each from Italy, Germany, the UK, and Dublin — so that together we adopt the founders moving over, make the introductions, and turn a hard first year into a fast one. It's collaborative by design: more of us, pointed at the exact migration I have the deepest edge on.

the events are the product — and the moat.

Content. For years I've published on internet culture, the creator economy, and now the frontier — most of it lives at internetculture.co, read by 7,000 subscribers on Substack and 20,000 followers on X: a highly curated audience of founders, creators, and investors. It reliably does two things: pulls deal flow toward me, and builds a public point of view that founders and co-investors reach for before they reach for most funds. A founder I've never met often already knows what I think before our first call, which means I tend to enter the relationship pre-trusted and pre-sorted. Content compounds while I sleep — it's the cheapest, most durable sourcing engine I have, and it has worked at every stage of my career.

VIII.

How the fund operates

Everything here is reverse-engineered from the edge — because fund size is your strategy has it backwards. The edge dictates the strategy; the size is the output. My edge is cultural, so I shouldn't lead rounds and don't deserve a lead's 10–15%; what I deserve is about 2% of the rounds I can get into early, priced between $5M and $30M. Two percent of those checks, across roughly 35 companies, is about $12M deployed — so the fund is $15M. Edge first, then ownership, then construction, then size.

$15M fund· ~$12M investable· ~35 companies· ~2% entry· 0 reserves· 10-year fund· 2% fees, front-loaded· US focus

And this is the right shape precisely because seed got expensive. The top of the market has tripled in a year, so the old emerging-manager playbook of only hunting $5M rounds is a value trap — cheap is usually cheap for a reason.

So I don't anchor to a low price; I anchor to ownership, and I flex on entry valuation when the company earns it. Being pre-consensus is what lets me hold 2% without paying the top-tier price — in before the round is hot, then doing the cultural work that prices it up. The construction follows from there.

CompaniesValuationCheckAllocationRole
25$5–15M~$200K$5Mpre-consensus — where the cultural mispricing lives (≈two-thirds of the book)
10$20–30M~$500K$5Mthe breakouts — I size up to keep ownership that matters
2$40–50M~$1M$2Mrare — a less pre-consensus founder whose first round is simply priced higher

Ownership is the constraint; everything else bends around it. Two-thirds of the time I'm getting 2% at $5–15M, where the mispricing lives; about a third of the time the founder is an obvious breakout and the round is hot, so I write more to hold the line; and on rare occasions I'll go up to ~$1M, usually for a founder who's a little less pre-consensus but whose first round is simply priced higher than the rest.

No reserves is deliberate, and the math is on its side. At pre-seed in capital-intensive companies the derisking between rounds is minimal, and if a company gets hot my pro-rata quickly exceeds anything a $15M fund could reserve anyway — so I'd run an SPV. Reallocating that capital into more early, high-ownership initial checks is simply the better construction for a fund this size. The one exception isn't really a reserve: if a founder caps my first check low and then, a few months in, offers me more allocation at a slightly higher mark because we've been working together, I'll take it — but that's the same bet, made twice.

Liquidity is the hardest part of this market, and entering first is how I get it. Exits have thinned out and realized returns are scarce. Coming in on the first round gives me a cost basis low enough to start taking secondary liquidity as early as Series B, when the best companies already have real secondary demand. I hold no board seats, so I'm free to sell when it's smart. I underwrite to returning the fund several times over through these partial sales as positions mature.

Ownership dilutes on a predictable path, and a single position returns the fund at valuations that are genuinely reachable:

StageMy ownershipOne position returns the fund at
Series B~1.3%~$1.2B
Series C~1.0%~$1.5B
Series D+~0.75%~$2.0B
pre-IPO / IPO~0.5%~$3.0B

The discipline is to bank a fund-returner the moment a position can deliver one: when selling half a stake would return the whole fund, I sell that half and let the rest ride for the asymmetric upside. A single company reaching ~$1.5B by its Series C — the kind that raises its B and C from the top funds — returns the entire fund from one position. Across 35 companies, a handful clearing that bar is a realistic base case. That is the case for the structure: enough shots to make the math forgiving, and enough ownership that the winners actually move the fund.

Sophisticated LPs raise a fair objection here: the concentrated book — ten to fifteen companies at fifteen percent each — is where a lot of smart money wants to be, on the logic that a small position is a rounding error. I don't offer that book, for a simple reason. Fifteen percent is a lead's number, bought by leading the round, taking the board seat, and carrying the company operationally — a different job, and a different edge, from mine. My edge is cultural, and priced honestly it's worth about two percent. Taking fifteen would mean pretending to be a lead I'm not, when I'd rather be the best in the world at the one thing I actually do.

And two percent works on its own terms. At the outcomes I underwrite to, two percent of a company is a real position — a single one reaching $1.5–3B returns the whole fund. Cultural value also scales where a board seat doesn't: a lead can serve a handful of boards well, while I can be the narrative engine for thirty-five. Breadth is the natural shape of this edge, and the secondary liquidity above is what turns it into returns.

concentrated by thesis, diversified by access — the right construction for a specialist cultural access fund.

The last piece is what lets the artisan model stretch to thirty-five companies without losing the in-person work: I've built a platform that scores deal flow from pre-funding signals shared by investors I trust, filtering roughly a thousand profiles a week down to the hundred I contact and the fifty I talk to — and it's getting more agentic up to the first call. The machine handles the funnel so the in-person work has room to happen.

IX.

The ask

I'm raising from a small number of people who want to be long-term partners in something deliberately small — the boutique it is now, kept that way for as long as I run it.

What I'm really looking for is people who get the game I'm playing. People who believe, the way I do, that a cultural edge will decide who gets onto the best cap tables over the next decade — and that the same edge is what the founders who matter need to build the next generation of empires, where the work is as much belief and storytelling as it is engineering. If that already reads as obvious to you, we are going to get along.

So take this as the start of something long. I'm unapologetic, relationship-first, mission-driven — with an open-minded read on where the future is going and a sharp cultural eye on how it gets there. The people this tends to fit are founders who made their own money, GPs who bring deal flow as well as capital, and family offices with long horizons. I want partners measured in years.

I'm starting to raise in the coming weeks, and I'd genuinely love to meet in person — around California or Europe. hugo@babylon.vc.
